title: Swing (politics)

infobox.value: (or **swing**) shows the extent of change in voter support, typically from one [election](/wiki/Election "Election") to another, expressed as a positive or negative percentage. A multi-party swing is an indicator of a change in the electorate's preference between candidates or parties, often between major parties in a [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system"). A swing can be calculated for the electorate as a whole, for a given electoral district or for a particular demographic.

**(Lab to Lib Dem)** **(Lib Dem to Con)** must be added where three parties stand. Otherwise, a problem when deciding which swing is meant and which swing is best to publish arises where a lower party takes first or second, or where a party loses one of the top two places.[[n 1]](#cite_note-8)

in the United States can refer to [swing states](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state"), those states that are known to shift an outcome between [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") and [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") parties, equivalent on a local level to [marginal seats](/wiki/Marginal_seat "Marginal seat"). By contrast, a non-swing state is the direct equivalent of a [safe seat](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat") as it rarely changes in outcome. The extent of change in political outcome is heavily influenced by the [voting system](/wiki/Voting_system "Voting system") in use.[[n 3]](#cite_note-10)

E.g. a [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") drops from first to third place and a [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") former runner-up wins to a [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") runner-up who was in third place, here three interesting swings are relevant: Lib Dem-Con, Lib Dem-Lab and Lab-Con (and their opposite sign (±) equivalents for the opposite way swings).

Britain, like the US, uses a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") [voting system](/wiki/Voting_system "Voting system") and where a new third party or independent candidate has a better or worse result (or stands or chooses not to) this calculation allows for a more understandable comparison between the parties which are of wider interest.
As in the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the US employs a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") voting system as its main voting system.

["Swingometer Map"](https://web.archive.org/web/20221022012130/http://ukpollingreport.co.uk/swingometer-map). *UK Polling Report*. Archived from [the original](http://ukpollingreport.co.uk/swingometer-map) on 22 October 2022.

Cowling, David (15 November 2019). ["Election 2017 guide: What is swing, how is it calculated and why is it important?"](https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/election-2017-guide-swing-what-is-it-how-calculate-important-electoral-expert-conservatives-labour-party-a7707321.html). *The Independent*.
Baker, Niamh; Uberoi, Elise (10 February 2023). ["Electoral Swing"](https://researchbriefings.files.parliament.uk/documents/SN02608/SN02608.pdf) (PDF). *Research briefings - UK Parliament*.

["Swingometer"](https://web.archive.org/web/20170612082424/http://www.bbc.co.uk/archive/swingometer/). *BBC*. Archived from [the original](https://www.bbc.co.uk/archive/swingometer) on 12 June 2017. Retrieved 25 December 2019.

McCormick, Mark; Ridley, Jenny; Dant, Alastair; Shuttleworth, Martin; Robinson, Michael (5 April 2010). ["Election map and swingometer"](https://www.theguardian.com/politics/interactive/2010/apr/05/general-election-map-swingometer). *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20230522025832/https://www.theguardian.com/politics/interactive/2010/apr/05/general-election-map-swingometer) from the original on 22 May 2023.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Change in voter support from one election to another

An **electoral swing analysis** (or **swing**) shows the extent of change in voter support, typically from one [election](/wiki/Election "Election") to another, expressed as a positive or negative percentage. A multi-party swing is an indicator of a change in the electorate's preference between candidates or parties, often between major parties in a [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system"). A swing can be calculated for the electorate as a whole, for a given electoral district or for a particular demographic.

A swing is particularly useful for analysing change in voter support over time, or as a tool for predicting the outcome of elections in [constituency](/wiki/Constituency "Constituency")-based systems. Swing is also usefully deployed when analysing the shift in voter intentions revealed by (political) [opinion polls](/wiki/Opinion_poll "Opinion poll") or to compare polls concisely which may rely on differing samples and on markedly different swings and therefore predict extraneous results.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Calculation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Calculation")]

The swing is calculated by comparing the percentage of the vote in a particular election to the percentage of the vote belonging to the same party or candidate at the previous election.

### One-party swing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: One-party swing")]

One-party swing (in [percentage points](/wiki/Percentage_point "Percentage point")) = Percentage of vote in current election − percentage of vote in previous election

For example, if a party had 41% of the vote in the previous election, and has 51% of the vote in the current election, the swing is 10 percentage points in their favour. This could be reported as +10 points.

### Two-party swing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Two-party swing")]

Two-party swing is calculated by taking the sum of the one-party swings of each party, then dividing by two.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In many nation states' media, including in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), swing is normally expressed in terms of two parties. This practice is most useful where most governments tend to be from an existing [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system"), and is sometimes used to try to predict the outcome of elections in constituency-based systems where different [seats](/wiki/Parliamentary_seat "Parliamentary seat") are held with different previous levels of support.

An assumption underlies extrapolated national calculations: that all districts will experience the same swing as shown in a poll or in a place's results. The advantage of this swing is the fact that the loss of support for one party will in most cases be accompanied by smaller or bigger gain in support for the other, but both figures are averaged into one. Employing the two assumptions allows the analyst to compute an [electoral pendulum](/wiki/Mackerras_pendulum "Mackerras pendulum"), predicting how many (and which) seats will change hands given a particular swing, and what size uniform swing would therefore bring about a change of government.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main article: [Swing (Australian politics)](/wiki/Swing_(Australian_politics) "Swing (Australian politics)")

In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the term "swing" refers to the change in the performance of a political party or candidate in an [election](/wiki/Election "Election") or opinion poll. As Australia uses the [preferential voting](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") system, swing can be expressed in terms of the primary vote (first preference vote), or in terms of the two-party-preferred (TPP) or two-candidate-preferred (TCP) result, which may represent significantly different values due to preference flows; i.e. a 5% primary vote swing does not necessarily represent an equivalent swing in TPP or TCP terms.

## United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [Swing (United Kingdom)](/wiki/Swing_(United_Kingdom) "Swing (United Kingdom)")

In the UK, a two-party swing (averaged model) is generally used, which adds one party's increase in share of the vote (expressed as a percentage point) to the percentage-point fall of another party, and divides the total by two.

Thus, if Party One's vote rises by 4 points and Party Two's vote falls 5 points, the swing is 4.5 points (Party 2 to Party 1).[[7]](#cite_note-7) For disambiguation, suffixes such as: **(Con to Lab)** **(Lab to Lib Dem)** **(Lib Dem to Con)** must be added where three parties stand. Otherwise, a problem when deciding which swing is meant and which swing is best to publish arises where a lower party takes first or second, or where a party loses one of the top two places.[[n 1]](#cite_note-8)

Originating as a mathematical calculation for comparing the results of two constituencies,[[n 2]](#cite_note-9) any of these figures can be used as an indication of the scale of voter change between any two political parties, as shown below for the [2010 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"):

* [![Labour to Conservative swing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/2010UKElectionLabConSwing.svg/120px-2010UKElectionLabConSwing.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2010UKElectionLabConSwing.svg "Labour to Conservative swing")

  Labour to Conservative swing
* [![Liberal Democrat to Conservative swing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/2010UKElectionLDConSwing.svg/120px-2010UKElectionLDConSwing.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2010UKElectionLDConSwing.svg "Liberal Democrat to Conservative swing")

  Liberal Democrat to Conservative swing
* [![Labour to Liberal Democrat swing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/2010UKElectionLabLDSwing.svg/120px-2010UKElectionLabLDSwing.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2010UKElectionLabLDSwing.svg "Labour to Liberal Democrat swing")

  Labour to Liberal Democrat swing

## United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Swing state](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state")

**Swing** in the United States can refer to [swing states](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state"), those states that are known to shift an outcome between [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") and [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") parties, equivalent on a local level to [marginal seats](/wiki/Marginal_seat "Marginal seat"). By contrast, a non-swing state is the direct equivalent of a [safe seat](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat") as it rarely changes in outcome. The extent of change in political outcome is heavily influenced by the [voting system](/wiki/Voting_system "Voting system") in use.[[n 3]](#cite_note-10)

## Three party swing or greater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Three party swing or greater")]

Some websites provide a [pie chart](/wiki/Pie_chart "Pie chart") based or column-based multi party [swingometer](/wiki/Swingometer "Swingometer") where ± x%, ± x%, ± x% and so on is displayed or can be input for three parties (or more in more plural [democracies](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy")). This tool or illustration provides likely outcomes wherever more than two [political parties](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") have a significant influence on which politicians are elected.[[8]](#cite_note-11) In multiparty systems [exit polls](/wiki/Exit_polls "Exit polls") frequently include a question as to voting behavior in the last applicable election. Based on those exit polls "voter migration" (where vote gains for a specific party came from and losses of another went to) analyses are published and discussed in election coverage.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Swing vote](/wiki/Swing_vote "Swing vote")
* [Swingometer](/wiki/Swingometer "Swingometer")

## Notes and references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swing_(politics)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Notes and references")]

Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** E.g. a [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") drops from first to third place and a [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") former runner-up wins to a [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") runner-up who was in third place, here three interesting swings are relevant: Lib Dem-Con, Lib Dem-Lab and Lab-Con (and their opposite sign (±) equivalents for the opposite way swings).
2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** Britain, like the US, uses a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") [voting system](/wiki/Voting_system "Voting system") and where a new third party or independent candidate has a better or worse result (or stands or chooses not to) this calculation allows for a more understandable comparison between the parties which are of wider interest.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** As in the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the US employs a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") voting system as its main voting system.

4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Baker, Niamh; Uberoi, Elise (10 February 2023). ["Electoral Swing"](https://researchbriefings.files.parliament.uk/documents/SN02608/SN02608.pdf) (PDF). *Research briefings - UK Parliament*.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** ["Electoral Calculus"](https://www.electoralcalculus.co.uk/homepage.html). *electoralcalculus.co.uk*.

7. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** ["Your questions answered"](https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/uk_news/politics/vote_2005/basics/4415923.stm). 7 April 2005 – via BBC News.
